skill.name: project-structure
skill.description: Organize project folders following industry best practices. Use when setting up new projects, reorganizing codebases, or when folder structure becomes messy. Covers Next.js, Bulletproof React, and FSD patterns.
skill.summary: Plan and reorganize project folders using practical patterns for general, Next.js, React, and FSD codebases.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, environment, and weak crypto patterns. Review found the network, environment, device-file, and weak-crypto hits are documentation false positives, while Bash examples for creating and moving folders create a real but bounded filesystem-change risk.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Reorganization Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Bash examples that create directories, move folders into _legacy, and scaffold project folders. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but incorrect use can move or create files in the user's workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and match the skill's stated filesystem organization purpose. They do not show malicious intent, but they can change user files if applied without review.

low_findings.title: Destructive Command Mentioned as a Negative Example
low_findings.description: The skill shows rm and rm -rf only inside a safety rule that forbids deletion and recommends moving files to _legacy instead. This appears to be a false positive for malicious deletion, but models should preserve the stated safety rule.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same section labels deletion as forbidden and provides a non-deleting alternative. The risk is low because the dangerous command is instructional contrast, not the recommended action.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Next.js, Bulletproof React, and Feature-Sliced Design references. No evidence found that the skill performs network requests or sends data externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are located in a References section and are not part of executable code. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or upload behavior in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive and Crypto Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The .env.example entry is a template filename, the ~/dev paths are directory examples, and FSD is an architecture acronym. No evidence found of secret access, device-file access, or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms are in directory trees, naming examples, or architecture labels. They are not code that reads secrets, opens device files, or computes hashes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash File Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill permits Bash and includes mkdir, mv, find, ls, and touch examples. These operations are expected for folder organization but should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly allows Bash and documents filesystem commands. The commands are not obfuscated and align with the skill's purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Move Targets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses date substitution when naming legacy folders. The substitution is fixed and not user-controlled, so command-injection risk is low.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is visible and limited to date formatting. I found no concatenation of untrusted input into shell commands.

content.value_statement: Messy folders make codebases harder to navigate and maintain. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan practical project structures and safe file moves.
actual_capabilities: Recommends developer root folder layouts for work, personal, study, open source, and playground projects.
Explains a basic project scaffold with source, assets, configuration, documentation, scripts, tests, and legacy folders.
Compares Next.js App Router, Bulletproof React, Feature-Sliced Design, and hybrid folder patterns.
Provides command examples for inspecting, creating, and reorganizing folders.
Documents naming conventions for folders, React components, hooks, utilities, and constants.
Encourages moving old files to _legacy instead of deleting them.
limitations: It focuses on folder structure and does not design application runtime architecture.
It includes Bash examples that should be reviewed before execution.
It does not inspect dependency graphs or enforce architectural boundaries automatically.
It provides general patterns and may need adaptation for non-frontend stacks.

use_cases.title: Start a New Frontend Project
use_cases.description: Choose a clear folder pattern before files spread across unrelated directories.

use_cases.title: Clean Up a Growing Codebase
use_cases.description: Move old folders aside and create a feature-oriented structure for easier maintenance.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Conventions
use_cases.description: Document folder naming and architecture choices for consistent pull requests.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Basic Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-structure skill to recommend a simple folder layout for my new project. Explain what each top-level folder should contain.

prompt_templates.title: Review Current Folders
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-structure skill to review my current folder tree. Identify confusing folders and suggest a safer target structure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a React Reorganization
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-structure skill to convert this React app toward a Bulletproof React style layout. Include safe move steps and legacy folder handling.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Architecture Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the project-structure skill to compare Next.js colocation, Bulletproof React, Feature-Sliced Design, and a hybrid approach for this codebase.

output_examples.input: I am starting a Next.js app with marketing pages and a dashboard.
output_examples.output: A recommended structure separates route groups for marketing and dashboard pages, keeps shared UI in common components, and places reusable helpers in a library folder.

output_examples.input: My React components folder has too many unrelated files.
output: Group authentication files under an auth feature.
Group user management files under a users feature.
Keep truly shared buttons and modals in a common components area.

output_examples.input: I need to clean up an old project without deleting files.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends creating a legacy area, moving old folders there with a date label, and scaffolding the new structure before moving active files.

best_practices: Review generated file-move commands before running them.
Choose one main classification rule, such as route, feature, or layer.
Move uncertain files to _legacy instead of deleting them during cleanup.
anti_patterns: Do not organize development roots only by programming language.
Do not keep active projects on the desktop or in temporary folders.
Do not place every component, hook, and utility in large type-only folders.

faq.question: Does this skill modify files automatically?
faq.answer: It can produce Bash and file-editing guidance when used with capable tools. Review every command before execution.

faq.question: Which project types does it cover best?
faq.answer: It is strongest for frontend and full-stack JavaScript projects, especially Next.js and React codebases.

faq.question: Can it help with Feature-Sliced Design?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains the FSD layers and when that pattern fits a team.

faq.question: Does it delete old folders?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly recommends moving old folders to _legacy instead of deleting them.

faq.question: Can it create a standard developer workspace?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends root folders for work, personal, study, open source, playground, and dotfiles projects.

faq.question: Is it a security or dependency audit tool?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on folder organization, naming conventions, and architecture layout guidance.
